definitions.definition: The outermost solid layer of the Earth, varying in thickness from 5-70 km, composed of rocks and minerals.
The layer beneath the crust, ranging in depth from 35 km to 2,900 km, composed of hot, viscous rock.
A liquid layer of iron and nickel, approximately 2,250 km thick, surrounding the inner core.
A solid, iron-nickel alloy at the center of the Earth, about 1,220 km in diameter.
A part of the Earth's crust that makes up the continents, thicker and less dense than oceanic crust.
A part of the Earth's crust that makes up the oceanic basins, thinner and denser than continental crust.
A boundary between the crust and the mantle, marking a change in seismic velocity and density.
Energy released by earthquakes that travels through the Earth, providing information about its internal structure.
An instrument used to record and measure the intensity of seismic waves.
Ruptures in the Earth's crust that release energy in the form of seismic waves.
Opening in the Earth's surface where magma and gases escape, often resulting in the eruption of lava and ash.
A mixture of molten rock, gases, and minerals beneath the Earth's surface.
The theory that the Earth's lithosphere is broken into moving plates that interact at their boundaries.
The outermost solid layer of the Earth, consisting of the crust and the uppermost part of the mantle.
A region of the upper mantle where the rock is partially molten and able to flow over long periods.
The movement of heat through the Earth's interior, driven by the rise of hot material and the sinking of cold material.
